answer:
In *Soul and Body II*, an anonymous Old English poem, the soul returns to reproach the decaying body it has left behind, visiting every seven nights for three hundred years (or until Judgment Day). It addresses the body with a long, bitter speech of accusation and lament, blaming the body for its sinful indulgences that have doomed the soul to torment.[^1]

The soul's words begin:

> “Listen, you sluggish thing, you dreary dust!  
> Why did you drag me down,  
> filth of the earth, all withered away,  
> likeness of loam! Little you thought  
> what your soul’s journey would later become,  
> once it was led from the body!  
> Behold, you blame me, accursed one! Listen, you food for worms,  
> little you thought how the Almighty Measurer, from His majesty,  
> sent a soul to you from the heavens above  
> through His own hand, and then redeemed you  
> with His holy blood, yet you bound me with hard hunger  
> and fettered me with the torments of hell![^2]

The soul continues, complaining of being trapped by the body's lusts, feasting, and pride while thirsting for God; prophesying the body's doom on Judgment Day; and cursing its former treasures and form as a human (preferring it had been a beast or worm instead). The speech culminates:

> But what shall we two do, when He has joined us again  
> a second time? Then we must together  
> forever endure such miseries as you prescribed for us.”[^3]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 10 “Soul and Body II” (/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/10-soul-and-body-ii/translation/).
[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 10 “Soul and Body II” [/paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/10-soul-and-body-ii/translation/#p-34bcc7).
[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 10 “Soul and Body II” [/paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/10-soul-and-body-ii/translation/#p-7f7247).
